summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the most fundamental structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?

Prokaryotic vs. eukaryotic: the most fundamental division in cellular life. Prokaryotes (Greek: pro = before, karyon = kernel/nucleus): no membrane-bound nucleus — DNA in nucleoid region; no membrane-bound organelles; typically 0.1-10 μm. Eukaryotes (eu = true): membrane-bound nucleus; compartmentalized organelles; typically 10-100 μm. Historical context: Édouard Chatton (1925) first proposed the prokaryote/eukaryote distinction. Carl Woese and George Fox (1977) used 16S/18S rRNA sequences to classify all life into three domains: Bacteria (prokaryotic), Archaea (prokaryotic), Eukarya (eukaryotic). Both cell types share: DNA as genetic material, ribosomes (different sizes), plasma membrane, cytoplasm. The distinction matters because: compartmentalization allows eukaryotes to be larger and more complex (organelles create specialized chemical environments).

What is Gram staining, and why is it one of the most important procedures in clinical microbiology?

Gram staining — cornerstone of clinical microbiology: (1) Procedure (Hans Christian Gram, 1884): apply crystal violet (primary stain) → iodine mordant (forms CV-I complex) → alcohol decolorization (critical step) → safranin counterstain. (2) Results: Gram-positive: thick peptidoglycan traps CV-I complex → alcohol cannot wash it out → purple. Examples: Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Bacillus, Clostridium. Gram-negative: thin peptidoglycan + outer membrane dissolved by alcohol → CV-I washed out → safranin counterstain → pink/red. Examples: E. coli, Salmonella, Neisseria, Pseudomonas. (3) Clinical significance: guides empiric antibiotic therapy before culture results return (takes 48-72 hrs). Gram-positive infections: penicillins, cephalosporins, vancomycin. Gram-negative infections: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, carbapenems. (4) Predicts pathogenic mechanisms: Gram-negative outer membrane contains LPS (endotoxin) → triggers severe inflammatory response (septic shock). Gram-positive: exotoxins (many), biofilm formation. (5) Limitations: Mycobacteria are acid-fast (waxy mycolic acid wall — need Ziehl-Neelsen stain). Mycoplasma: no cell wall → cannot be Gram-stained. Still, >130 years later, Gram staining remains the first diagnostic test performed on most clinical bacterial specimens.

What is the endosymbiotic theory, and how does it explain the evolutionary relationship between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?

Endosymbiotic theory — bridging prokaryotes and eukaryotes: (1) Proposed by Lynn Margulis (1967, initially rejected by 15 journals). Core idea: eukaryotic organelles arose from endosymbiosis (one cell living inside another). (2) Sequence: ancestral eukaryote (large anaerobic archaeon?) engulfed aerobic α-proteobacterium (~2 BYA) → not digested → mutualistic relationship → aerobe provided ATP from O₂ → host provided nutrients and protection → aerobe became mitochondrion. Later, some mitochondria-containing cells engulfed cyanobacteria → cyanobacterium became chloroplast (~1.5 BYA) → gave rise to plant/algae lineages. (3) Evidence: see L7.5 Q27 for comprehensive list (double membranes, circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, binary fission, phylogenetics, gene transfer to nucleus). (4) Ongoing evolution: most original endosymbiont genes transferred to host nucleus over billions of years (endosymbiotic gene transfer). Mitochondria retain only ~37 genes (humans). Chloroplasts retain ~120 genes. Must import ~1,000-1,500 nuclear-encoded proteins through TOM/TIM (mitochondria) or TOC/TIC (chloroplasts) complexes. (5) Present-day examples: Paulinella chromatophora (photosynthetic amoeba with 'chromatophore' acquired from cyanobacteria ~60 MYA — endosymbiosis in progress). (6) Significance: endosymbiosis was the key step that allowed eukaryotic cellular complexity — mitochondrial ATP production provided the energy budget for larger genomes and greater cellular complexity.

A patient develops a persistent catheter-associated urinary tract infection that resists multiple rounds of antibiotic treatment. Microscopy reveals bacteria embedded in a dense matrix of polysaccharides, proteins, and DNA on the catheter surface. What is this structure, and why does it make infections so difficult to treat?

Biofilms — prokaryotic community living: (1) Structure: bacteria attach to surface → produce EPS (extracellular polymeric substance) matrix → form 3D architecture with water channels (nutrient delivery) → mature biofilm with microcolonies. EPS composition: polysaccharides, proteins, extracellular DNA (eDNA — structural scaffold). (2) Resistance mechanisms: physical barrier (EPS prevents antibiotic penetration — slow diffusion, adsorption, enzymatic degradation within matrix), metabolic heterogeneity (outer bacteria — active, susceptible; inner bacteria — nutrient-deprived, slow metabolism → antibiotics targeting DNA replication, protein synthesis, cell wall synthesis are less effective on non-growing cells), persister cells (stochastically dormant subpopulation — survive antibiotic exposure → regrow when antibiotic removed → relapsing infections), horizontal gene transfer (close cell proximity → conjugation rates up to 1,000× higher within biofilm → rapid spread of resistance genes). (3) Clinical significance: ~80% of chronic bacterial infections involve biofilms: catheter infections (UTI, central line), dental plaque (caries, periodontal disease), lung infections in cystic fibrosis (Pseudomonas aeruginosa), prosthetic joint/implant infections, chronic wounds, endocarditis (heart valve). Treatment: often requires physical removal (catheter removal/replacement) + high-dose combination antibiotic therapy. (4) Quorum sensing: bacteria in biofilms communicate via chemical signals (autoinducers) → coordinate gene expression as a community (quorum sensing — discovered by Bonnie Bassler et al.).

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Horizontal gene transfer (HGT) and antibiotic resistance: (1) Three mechanisms (unique to prokaryotes — no eukaryotic equivalent of this scale): (a) Transformation: competent bacteria uptake naked DNA from environment (released by dead cells). Natural competence in some species (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, B. subtilis). Griffith's experiment (1928) demonstrated transformation. (b) Conjugation: requires cell-to-cell contact. F plasmid encodes sex pilus → bridges donor (F⁺) to recipient (F⁻) → plasmid copy transferred → recipient becomes F⁺. Can transfer chromosomal genes (Hfr = high frequency recombination, when F integrates into chromosome). R plasmids (resistance) spread via conjugation — major clinical concern. (c) Transduction: bacteriophage packages host DNA fragments during lysis → injects into new host. Generalized (random host DNA) or specialized (specific genes near phage integration site). (2) MRSA: mecA gene on SCCmec (Staphylococcal Cassette Chromosome mec — a mobile genetic element). PBP2a: altered penicillin-binding protein with low β-lactam affinity → transpeptidase activity continues despite β-lactam presence → cell wall synthesis not inhibited. Treatment: vancomycin (Gram-positive → no outer membrane barrier), daptomycin, linezolid. VRSA (vancomycin-resistant) now emerging. (3) Global crisis: WHO lists antibiotic resistance as 'one of the biggest threats to global health' — ~700,000 deaths/year from resistant infections (projected ~10 million by 2050). HGT enables resistance to spread between species — unlike eukaryotic vertical (parent-to-offspring) inheritance.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What advantage does compartmentalization (membrane-bound organelles) provide to eukaryotic cells that prokaryotic cells lack?

Compartmentalization advantage: (1) Specialized environments: lysosomes (pH 4.5 for hydrolysis), ER lumen (oxidizing for disulfide bonds), mitochondrial matrix (enzymes for citric acid cycle), peroxisomes (catalase for H₂O₂ detoxification). (2) Simultaneous incompatible reactions: protein synthesis (cytoplasm) AND protein degradation (lysosomes) can occur in the same cell without interference. (3) Increased surface area: cristae in mitochondria, thylakoids in chloroplasts — membranes increase area for membrane-bound reactions (ETC, photosystems). (4) Larger cell volume: prokaryotes are limited by surface area:volume ratio (all metabolic reactions occur at/near the plasma membrane). Eukaryotes overcome this with internal membranes that increase total reactive surface area. (5) Regulatory control: nuclear envelope separates transcription from translation — allows mRNA processing (splicing, capping, polyadenylation) before export — additional layer of gene regulation absent in prokaryotes. (6) Evolutionary consequence: eukaryotic compartmentalization (especially mitochondrial endosymbiosis) enabled the ~1,000-fold increase in genome size and cellular complexity that distinguishes eukaryotes from prokaryotes.

Example: How do prokaryotic flagella fundamentally differ from eukaryotic flagella in structure and mechanism of movement?

Flagella comparison — convergent evolution: (1) Prokaryotic flagellum: Structure: hollow tube of flagellin protein subunits (~20 nm diameter). Components: filament (flagellin), hook (flexible coupling), basal body (motor embedded in membrane/wall — stator and rotor rings). Mechanism: rotary motor powered by proton motive force (H⁺ flow through MotA/MotB stator). Speed: 100-1,000+ rpm. Direction: counterclockwise rotation → flagella bundle → 'run' (forward movement). Clockwise → flagella splay apart → 'tumble' (random reorientation). Chemotaxis: bacteria sense chemical gradients → adjust run/tumble frequency. (2) Eukaryotic flagellum/cilium: Structure: 9+2 axoneme — 9 outer doublet microtubules + 2 central singlets. Made of α/β-tubulin. ~250 nm diameter. Enclosed in plasma membrane extension. Mechanism: dynein motor proteins (molecular motors) cross-bridge between adjacent doublets → sliding → produces bending wave. Powered by ATP hydrolysis. Cilia: short, numerous, coordinated wavelike beating (e.g., respiratory epithelium). Flagella: long, few, undulating motion (e.g., sperm). (3) NOT homologous — completely different molecular composition, energy source, and mechanism. Same function (motility) arrived at independently = convergent evolution.

Example: Which definition BEST describes a flagellum?

Flagellum — motility appendage with two distinct forms: (1) Prokaryotic flagellum: protein: flagellin. Structure: filament + hook + basal body (motor: rotor and stator rings embedded in membrane/wall). Energy: proton motive force (H⁺ gradient, or Na⁺ in marine bacteria). Mechanism: rotary motor — counterclockwise = 'run' (flagella bundle, forward swimming); clockwise = 'tumble' (flagella splay, random reorientation). Speed: up to 100+ body lengths/second. Chemotaxis: biased random walk toward attractants/away from repellents (mediated by MCPs → CheA/CheY signaling → flagellar motor switch). (2) Eukaryotic flagellum/cilium: protein: tubulin (α/β-tubulin heterodimers). Structure: 9+2 axoneme (9 outer doublets + 2 central singlets), nexin links, radial spokes, dynein arms. Covered by plasma membrane. Energy: ATP (dynein motor proteins). Mechanism: dynein walking causes doublet sliding → bending (restricted by nexin links). Cilia: short, numerous, coordinated beating (respiratory epithelium — mucociliary clearance). Flagella: long, few, undulating (sperm tail). (3) Key point: NOT homologous — completely independent origins. Same function arrived at by convergent evolution. Prokaryotic flagellar motor has been called 'the most efficient molecular machine known' — converts ~100% of input energy to output rotation.

Example: A hospital microbiology lab receives a blood culture from a patient with high fever and suspected sepsis. The Gram stain shows Gram-negative rods. The physician immediately changes the antibiotic regimen from vancomycin to a broad-spectrum Gram-negative agent. Why does the Gram stain result change the treatment strategy, and what specific threats does a Gram-negative infection pose?

Gram stain in clinical decision-making: (1) Why vancomycin fails against Gram-negatives: vancomycin (MW ~1,449 Da) works by binding D-Ala-D-Ala at the end of peptidoglycan precursor peptides → blocks cross-linking → cell wall synthesis fails → lysis. For Gram-positives: thick peptidoglycan exposed → vancomycin accesses target easily. For Gram-negatives: outer membrane acts as permeability barrier → vancomycin too large to penetrate porins (typically allow molecules <600-700 Da) → cannot reach peptidoglycan target. (2) Appropriate Gram-negative antibiotics: aminoglycosides (gentamicin — enter through porins + self-promoted uptake), fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin — penetrate porins), β-lactams with outer membrane penetration (aztreonam — monobactam), carbapenems (meropenem — small enough + resistant to most β-lactamases). (3) LPS/endotoxin threat: Lipid A component of LPS → triggers TLR4 on macrophages → massive cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) → systemic inflammatory response (SIRS) → sepsis → septic shock (vasodilation, hypotension, organ failure) → mortality 30-50%. Even dead Gram-negative bacteria release LPS → antibiotic-mediated bacterial killing can initially worsen sepsis (Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction). (4) Time is critical in sepsis: every hour of delayed appropriate antibiotics increases mortality by ~7.6%. The Gram stain provides actionable information within 15-30 minutes — vs. culture (48-72 hrs).

Example: A patient develops a persistent catheter-associated urinary tract infection that resists multiple rounds of antibiotic treatment. Microscopy reveals bacteria embedded in a dense matrix of polysaccharides, proteins, and DNA on the catheter surface. What is this structure, and why does it make infections so difficult to treat?

Biofilms — prokaryotic community living: (1) Structure: bacteria attach to surface → produce EPS (extracellular polymeric substance) matrix → form 3D architecture with water channels (nutrient delivery) → mature biofilm with microcolonies. EPS composition: polysaccharides, proteins, extracellular DNA (eDNA — structural scaffold). (2) Resistance mechanisms: physical barrier (EPS prevents antibiotic penetration — slow diffusion, adsorption, enzymatic degradation within matrix), metabolic heterogeneity (outer bacteria — active, susceptible; inner bacteria — nutrient-deprived, slow metabolism → antibiotics targeting DNA replication, protein synthesis, cell wall synthesis are less effective on non-growing cells), persister cells (stochastically dormant subpopulation — survive antibiotic exposure → regrow when antibiotic removed → relapsing infections), horizontal gene transfer (close cell proximity → conjugation rates up to 1,000× higher within biofilm → rapid spread of resistance genes). (3) Clinical significance: ~80% of chronic bacterial infections involve biofilms: catheter infections (UTI, central line), dental plaque (caries, periodontal disease), lung infections in cystic fibrosis (Pseudomonas aeruginosa), prosthetic joint/implant infections, chronic wounds, endocarditis (heart valve). Treatment: often requires physical removal (catheter removal/replacement) + high-dose combination antibiotic therapy. (4) Quorum sensing: bacteria in biofilms communicate via chemical signals (autoinducers) → coordinate gene expression as a community (quorum sensing — discovered by Bonnie Bassler et al.).

Example: Robert Koch (Nobel Prize 1905) established his famous postulates to prove that a specific microorganism causes a specific disease. He used these criteria to identify Mycobacterium tuberculosis as the causative agent of tuberculosis and Bacillus anthracis for anthrax. What are Koch's postulates, and what are their modern limitations?

Koch's postulates — foundation of medical microbiology: (1) Robert Koch (1843-1910): German physician. Identified causative agents of: anthrax (Bacillus anthracis, 1876), tuberculosis (Mycobacterium tuberculosis, 1882), cholera (Vibrio cholerae, 1883). Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1905. (2) Postulates (formalized 1884): (a) Association: pathogen present in all disease cases, absent from healthy hosts. (b) Isolation: pathogen isolated from diseased host, grown in pure culture. (c) Causation: pure culture causes disease in new susceptible host. (d) Re-isolation: same pathogen re-isolated from experimentally infected host. (3) Limitations: unculturable pathogens (Mycobacterium leprae — causes leprosy — never grown in pure culture; Treponema pallidum — syphilis), asymptomatic carriers (Salmonella typhi — 'Typhoid Mary' — healthy carrier), ethical constraints (cannot inoculate humans with deadly pathogens), polymicrobial diseases (multiple organisms required), viral diseases (Koch worked before virology matured), host factors (genetic susceptibility, immune status). (4) Modern adaptations: molecular Koch's postulates (Stanley Falkow, 1988): (a) virulence gene present in pathogenic strains, absent from avirulent; (b) gene inactivation → loss of virulence; (c) restoration of gene → restores virulence. Used with: transposon mutagenesis, gene knockouts, complementation studies. (5) Legacy: Koch's work established germ theory on solid experimental ground and created the template for identifying causative agents of infectious diseases.
